festivals.name: Tuesday (Tyr's Day)
festivals.description: Named after Tyr in Germanic languages.

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Fenrir
keyMyths.description: Only Tyr had the courage to place his hand in Fenrir's mouth as a pledge of good faith. When Fenrir realized he could not break free, he bit off Tyr's hand at the wrist.

mythologyDetails.overview: Tyr is one of the oldest gods in the Norse pantheon, possibly predating Odin as a chief war deity. He represents the lawful and honorable aspects of warfare, in contrast to Odin's more cunning approach.
mythologyDetails.significance: The one-handed god of law, justice, and honorable combat who sacrificed his sword hand to bind the monstrous wolf Fenrir.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Tyr embodies the concept of fairness and upholding the law.

associations.name: Sacrifice
associations.significance: His willingness to sacrifice his hand demonstrates his commitment to the greater good.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Symbolizes his one-handedness and unwavering commitment.

associations.name: Red
associations.significance: Represents courage, war, and justice, fitting Tyr's role.

associations.name: Mars
associations.significance: Associated with war and courage, reflecting Tyr's martial aspects.

associations.name: Wolf
associations.significance: Connected to the binding of Fenrir, a pivotal event in Tyr's mythos.

worshipPractices.name: Oath-taking
worshipPractices.description: Oaths were sworn in Tyr's name, emphasizing the binding nature of promises and commitments. Breaking an oath to Tyr was considered a grave offense.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Animals, particularly those associated with war and strength, were sacrificed to Tyr to seek his favor in legal matters and battles. Weapons may also have been offered.

festivals.name: Týsdagr
festivals.description: The day of the week Tuesday is named after Tyr, suggesting a weekly recognition of his importance in Norse society.

festivals.name: Thing gatherings
festivals.description: While not exclusively for Tyr, legal assemblies (Things) likely involved invocations or recognition of Tyr as the god of law and justice.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tyr's legacy endures in the continued emphasis on justice, oaths, and the rule of law in modern society. He is also a figure of interest in contemporary paganism and Asatru.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Tyr's parentage is somewhat ambiguous in the surviving Norse texts. He is generally considered to be a son of Odin, the Allfather, though some sources suggest a connection to the giant Hymir. This ambiguity reflects the complex relationships between gods and giants in Norse cosmology. Regardless of his specific parentage, Tyr's association with law and justice sets him apart from many other deities primarily associated with war or fertility. His role as a god of order within a world often threatened by chaos is a key aspect of his character. From an early age, Tyr was likely revered for his courage and his commitment to upholding the law, traits that would define his later actions and sacrifices. His upbringing would have emphasized the importance of honor, oaths, and the defense of the community against external threats. This foundational understanding of justice and duty shaped his destiny and solidified his position as a protector of the Norse people.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most famous myth involving Tyr is the binding of the monstrous wolf Fenrir. The gods feared Fenrir's growing power and sought to restrain him. After two failed attempts with unbreakable chains, they enlisted the help of the dwarves, who forged a magical ribbon called Gleipnir. Gleipnir appeared soft and harmless, but was made of impossible ingredients. Suspecting treachery, Fenrir demanded that one of the gods place their hand in his mouth as a guarantee of good faith. Only Tyr was brave enough to accept this challenge. When the gods refused to release Fenrir, Gleipnir tightened, and Fenrir bit off Tyr's hand. This act of self-sacrifice ensured the safety of the gods and delayed Ragnarok, the end of the world. Tyr's loss is a testament to his unwavering commitment to justice and the greater good. He knowingly sacrificed himself to protect the Aesir, even though it meant enduring permanent physical impairment. His courage and selflessness in this myth cemented his reputation as a hero and a symbol of unwavering dedication to duty.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Evidence of Tyr's worship is found throughout Scandinavia, particularly in place names and runic inscriptions. 'Tysdagr,' the Old Norse term for Tuesday, is named after him, indicating a widespread recognition of his importance. While specific temples dedicated solely to Tyr are rare in archaeological records, it is likely that he was honored at communal gatherings and legal assemblies (Things). Oaths were sworn in his name, underscoring the sacredness of promises and agreements in Norse society. Breaking an oath sworn to Tyr was considered a grave offense, carrying both social and spiritual consequences. Sacrifices, likely including animals and possibly weapons, were offered to Tyr to seek his favor in legal disputes and battles. His association with justice and warfare made him a crucial deity for those seeking resolution to conflicts and success in combat. The prominence of Tyr's name in runic inscriptions also suggests his role as a protector and a source of strength for individuals and communities.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tyr's legacy extends beyond the Viking Age, influencing legal and ethical concepts in subsequent cultures. His association with justice, oaths, and heroic sacrifice continues to resonate in modern society. He remains a significant figure in contemporary paganism and Asatru, where he is revered as a god of law, order, and courage. The concept of upholding one's word, a central theme in Tyr's mythology, remains a cornerstone of ethical behavior in many societies. His self-sacrifice for the greater good serves as an enduring example of heroism and dedication to duty. In popular culture, Tyr often appears in literature, video games, and films, representing themes of justice, sacrifice, and the importance of upholding moral principles. His one-handedness has become a symbol of strength in the face of adversity and a reminder that true courage lies in doing what is right, even when it comes at a great personal cost. Tyr's story continues to inspire and challenge individuals to strive for justice and to honor their commitments, even in the face of overwhelming odds.

shortDescription: Tyr is the Norse god of law, justice, oaths, and heroic glory, known for sacrificing his hand to bind the wolf Fenrir.
subtitle: The One-Handed God of Law and Heroic Glory
